retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is a medication used to manage and treat a variety of inflammatory conditions, including psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease. It is in the monoclonal antibody class of medications. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for ustekinumab as a valuable agent in treating inflammatory diseases. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for members of the interprofessional team in the management of psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the mechanism of action of ustekinumab. Describe the indications for the use of ustekinumab. Review the potential adverse event that can accompany ustekinumab use. Outline strategies utilized by healthcare teams to improve care coordination and communication when ustekinumab is being administered. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Psoriatic Arthritis -- Treatment Planning -- Ustekinumab (IL12/23i)
retrieved_docs.content: Human mab to IL-12/23 p40 subunit Dose: 45 mg SC every 3 months after loading (90 mg if weight >100 kg) FDA-approved for psoriatic arthritis, psoriasis, and Crohn’s disease

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is a human monoclonal antibody typically used to treat moderate to severe plaque psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, moderate to severe Crohn disease, or moderate to severe ulcerative colitis (inflammatory bowel disease). Ustekinumab mediates the body's T-cell response by acting as an antagonist against interleukin-12 (IL12) and interleukin-23 (IL23). The drug is FDA approved for use in moderate to severe plaque psoriasis (Ps), particularly in those patients who are candidates for phototherapy or systemic therapy, aged 6 or older. Patients with active psoriatic arthritis (PsA) may use ustekinumab as monotherapy or in combination with methotrexate.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of inflammatory diseases such as psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease is complex and associated with a high-cost burden. The rational and cost-effective use of biological medications, such as ustekinumab, is a key component in managing these diseases. To achieve the desired outcome, an interprofessional healthcare team approach is necessary. Internal medicine clinicians (including mid-level practitioners such as nurse practitioners and physician assistants) may work with specialists, including rheumatologists, gastroenterologists, or dermatologists, to confirm the diagnosis and decide on a treatment course. Nurses facilitate the preparation and administration of medications, as well as monitoring patients after use; they should immediately report any concerns to the ordering/prescribing clinician. Pharmacists can ensure no relevant drug-drug interactions exist, evaluate the relevance of contraindications to treatment, verify dosing is correct (especially inasmuch as ustekinumab utilizes weight-based dosing regimens), and ensure the appropriateness of prescriptions. All clinical personnel need to participate in patient counseling, educate the patient regarding potential adverse events, signs of therapeutic failure, and provide access to resources and points of contact should they need to reach out to the interprofessional team. Social workers identify barriers to compliance and provide resources to patients. Each member of the interprofessional team's contributions can provide expertise from their discipline to help drive a positive outcome for patients with potentially fewer adverse events and improve the effectiveness of the broader healthcare system. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage and Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis treatment are based on an initial intravenous weight-based infusion followed by a subcutaneous maintenance schedule. [22] [23] Initial infusion dosage: Weight < 55 kg: 260 mg (2 vials) Weight 55 to 85 kg : 390 mg (3 vials) Weight > 85 kg: 520 mg (4 vials) Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis recommended adult S/C maintenance dosage: 90 mg subcutaneously eight weeks after initial intravenous administration and every eight weeks after that. Renal dosing (including both hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis) and hepatic dosing are undefined.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is available for injection in pre-filled syringes and vials. The drug is administered by either subcutaneous injection or intravenous infusion. The dosage and administration recommendations depend on the particular indication for treatment and the patient's weight.

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: There have been reports of the rapid development of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma in those patients treated with ustekinumab. These patients often had risk factors for developing this type of cancer, such as being older (greater than 60) and having undergone phototherapy or other immunosuppressant treatment. Patients should be monitored closely for the development of non-melanoma skin cancers. [37] As with all biological drugs, live vaccines should not be administered while on ustekinumab treatment. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Psoriasis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab is the first line biological agent of choice. Secukinumab is another alternative

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Adverse Effects -- Common Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Nasopharyngitis Headache Fatigue Vomiting after induction in inflammatory bowel disease Injection site erythema Bronchitis/upper respiratory tract infection Sinusitis Abdominal pain, fever, influenza, and diarrhea while used for maintenance therapy in inflammatory bowel disease Back pain

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Ustekinumab can be used during pregnancy. Based on limited human data, there is no expected risk of fetal harm, and teratogenicity has not been demonstrated in animal studies at up to 100 times the maximum recommended human dose (MRHD). The drug may also be used during breastfeeding; no human data is currently available, but there is no expectation of infant harm based on the drug's properties. No human data exist regarding ustekinumab's effect on milk production. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage and Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Psoriasis adult S/C recommended dosage: Less than or equal to 100 kg: 45 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks Greater than 100 kg: 90 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks. [18] Psoriatic arthritis adult S/C recommended dosage: Less than or equal to 100 kg: 45 mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks Greater than 100 kg with co-existent moderate-severe plaque psoriasis: 90mg administered subcutaneously initially, followed by another injection four weeks later and then every 12 weeks. [19] [20] Psoriasis pediatric patients recommended subcutaneous dosage: Doses are administered initially, four weeks later, and then every 12 weeks. [21] Weight < 60 kg: 0.75 mg/kg subcutaneously Weight 60 to 100 kg: 45 mg subcutaneously Weight > 100 kg: 90 mg subcutaneously

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Administration -- Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Subcutaneous injection: Injection: 45 mg/0.5 mL or 90mg/mL solution in a pre-filled syringe Injection: 45 mg/0.5 mL solution in a single-dose vial Intravenous infusion: Injection: 130 mg/26 mL (5 mg/mL) in a single-dose vial

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: As the development of biosimilars agents has progressed over the years, awareness in subspecialty practices encountering high incidence and prevalence of inflammatory diseases such as rheumatology, dermatology, and gastroenterology is also expanding. [4] Other specialties such as oncology and endocrinology have now also introduced biotherapeutics. [4] However, a gap in knowledge and confusion of biosimilars may exist in non-subspecialty practices, particularly with primary care clinicians (PCP). While PCP's are up to date on the more conventional original biologic treatments, many may not have gained familiarity and experience with the more current biosimilar innovative therapeutics, which in turn are more inexpensive, further increasing medication acquisition and compliance improving disease outcomes and the quality of life. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Upadacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity offers an in-depth examination of upadacitinib, a second-generation selective Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor targeting the JAK1 enzyme, approved by the FDA for treating various chronic inflammatory conditions such as moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis, atopic dermatitis, ankylosing spondylitis, Crohn disease, and ulcerative colitis. The session will cover the specific mechanism by which upadacitinib inhibits key intracellular cytoplasmic enzymes (Janus kinases) involved in immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, disrupting the JAK-STAT signaling pathway and thereby reducing inflammatory responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 450
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing products by their potency and intended route of administration. This integrated classification supports precise prescribing, formulation selection, and patient‑centered management of inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2151
retrieved_docs.content: Antiastmatika, a class of anti‑asthmatic agents, includes betamethason as a specific member of this therapeutic group. Although primarily recognized for its anti‑asthmatic properties, betamethason is also employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), illustrating its dual utility in both respiratory and dermatologic treatment. This overlap underscores the importance for clinicians to consider betamethason’s broader therapeutic spectrum when addressing patients who may have concurrent asthma and psoriasis, thereby supporting more comprehensive and individualized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions, such as severe asthma, and they share a common therapeutic classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 459
retrieved_docs.content: Fluokortolon and desoximetason are both classified as potent corticosteroids and are included in the category “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations.” As members of this group, they share the pharmacologic characteristics of strong glucocorticoid agents, providing powerful anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and often being incorporated into various combination drug formulations. Recognizing their membership in this potent corticosteroid class guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapeutic regimens and anticipating the potency‑related safety considerations associated with high‑strength glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1471
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب اللويحي (multiple sclerosis) may be treated with triamcinolon, a drug that belongs to the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids, represented by both triamcinolon and rimexolone, share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are employed in the management of various inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Recognizing that triamcinolon and rimexolone are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group informs clinicians in selecting appropriate steroid therapy for conditions such as multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids classified as potent (group III) comprise the agents amcinonide, flutikason, budesonide, fluocinolonacetonide and fluocinonide, each of which is a member of this high‑potency corticosteroid group. These drugs share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are commonly employed in dermatologic, respiratory and other inflammatory conditions where a potent corticosteroid effect is required. Their classification together reflects similar pharmacologic strength, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy for patients needing potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 544
retrieved_docs.content: Aminosalicylic acid and similar agents are classified as members of the mesalamin group and function as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Antiallergic intestinal antiinflammatory drugs, excluding corticosteroids, are also categorized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Additionally, corticosteroids acting locally are recognized as intestinal antiinflammatory agents. Together, these drug classes—mesalamin derivatives, antiallergic non‑corticosteroid agents, and locally acting corticosteroids—share the therapeutic role of reducing inflammation within the gastrointestinal tract, highlighting the diverse pharmacologic approaches available for managing intestinal inflammatory conditions.
